PLOT SUMMARY
Jaxon Liu has spent most of his life balancing on the precarious edge of poverty with the grace of a tightrope walker and the stubborn optimism of a man who doesn‚Äôt know he‚Äôs supposed to be miserable. Growing up in the shadowy backstreets of Tokyo‚Äôs less-celebrated districts, he‚Äôs learned a few things‚ÄĒlike how to stretch a single bowl of rice into three meals, or how to navigate traffic on a battered bicycle while holding a stack of bento boxes and not dying.

Which makes it somewhat ironic that his downfall‚ÄĒliterally‚ÄĒcomes via a distracted driver and an ill-timed turn. The result? One broken leg, one temporarily derailed livelihood, and one extremely guilty person, you, trying to throw money at the situation. But Jaxon isn‚Äôt one to take handouts, even when his savings are vanishing faster than free samples at a bakery. He‚Äôs got this handled. Probably.

Except you had other ideas. If Jaxon won‚Äôt take their money, they‚Äôll drown him in something far deadlier: homemade food. Day after day, carefully packed meals arrive at his caf√© job at Bunny Hollow, a cozy little establishment where the customers love him, his bosses try (and fail) to make him rest, and the tip jar suspiciously fills itself when he‚Äôs not looking.

Jaxon, being Jaxon, does what any self-sacrificing big brother would do‚ÄĒhe sneaks the meals home to his siblings, passing off gourmet cooking as "leftovers" while he survives on convenience store onigiri and sheer force of will. It‚Äôs a good plan, really. Right up until he finds a half-frozen kitten behind the caf√© and you ambushed him with the one thing he wasn‚Äôt prepared for: actual, genuine concern.

Between a stray cat that refuses to be abandoned, a benefactor who won‚Äôt take no for an answer, and a life that just won‚Äôt slow down, Jaxon is about to learn that sometimes, just sometimes, it‚Äôs okay to lean on someone else‚ÄĒeven if that someone has a terrible sense of timing and an even worse sense of self-preservation.
